\subsection{Task Execution Quality Evaluation}\label{sec:eval_task_execution}

Task execution is a widely adopted evaluation criterion because it provides a direct measure of model success on the task.
However, binary success rates are easy to interpret, but they cannot distinguish early failure from near-complete but unstable execution, especially in long-horizon or bimanual settings. 
Finer-grained metrics can be grouped into three categories.
Task progression metrics are more closely aligned with \(\mathcal{K}_2\)-level task organization, trajectory-quality metrics probe \(\mathcal{K}_1\)-level execution quality, and bimanual coordination metrics reflect execution coupling.
For task completion, RoboEval~\cite{wang2025roboeval} decomposes manipulation into sequential stages and measures progress as the fraction completed, 
while REALM~\cite{sedlacek2025realm} refines this idea by weighting stages according to their importance.
\begin{equation}
TP = \sum_{k=1}^K w_k s_k, \qquad \sum_{k=1}^K w_k = 1
\end{equation}
where $s_k \in {0,1}$ indicates whether stage $k$ succeeds, and $w_k$ captures its relative importance. REALM also evaluates robustness under perturbations through Root Mean Square Deviation.
For trajectory quality, smoothness metrics such as SPARC~\cite{balasubramanian2015sparc} and LDLJ~\cite{antonelli2023movement} quantify jerk and motion regularity, while Dynamic Time Warping measures alignment with expert demonstrations under temporal misalignment. 
For bimanual manipulation, RoboEval also measures coordination through velocity divergence and height discrepancy between end effectors.

\subsection{Data Efficiency and Quality Evaluation}\label{sec:eval_data_sample}

Data efficiency and quality concern whether the structured record \(\mathcal{D}\) is sufficiently informative and efficient to induce useful \(\mathcal{K}\).
Existing evaluation efforts in this direction are typically conducted along three dimensions.
One dimension is whether a dataset provides broad and non-redundant coverage. 
DemInf~\cite{hejna2025robot} approaches this question by measuring the marginal contribution of each demonstration to state-action coverage. 
Another dimension is whether demonstrations still useful for downstream learning. 
CUPID~\cite{agia2025cupidcuratingdatarobot} estimates how individual demonstrations affect downstream policy performance, providing a way to rank training data by utility. 
QoQ~\cite{lee2026qoq} instead evaluates samples by their contribution to validation improvement, helping identify low-quality or detrimental demonstrations.
Data efficiency is commonly summarized by the Area Under the Learning Curve:
\begin{equation}
  \text{AULC} = \int_0^{N_{\max}} SR(n)\, dn \approx \sum_{k} SR(n_k) \cdot \Delta n_k
\end{equation}
where $SR(n)$ is the success rate after training on $n$ demonstrations. AULC is more informative than reporting final success after a fixed data budget, since it captures how quickly useful capability emerges.

\subsection{Generalization and Transfer Evaluation}\label{sec:eval_benchmarking}

Generalization and transfer evaluation concern whether the knowledge support \(\mathcal{K}\) learned from the structured record \(\mathcal{D}\), and organized through \(B\) and \(P\), remains valid under distribution shift and varying embodiment conditions. Existing efforts in this direction can be grouped into three categories: human judgment, robustness under distribution shift, and proxy validity.
Human judgment evaluates aspects of manipulation quality that are not fully reflected by automated success metrics. RoboArena~\cite{atreya2025roboarena} uses pairwise human comparison to infer global policy rankings across distributed real-world evaluations, while AutoEval~\cite{zhou2025autoeval} reduces annotation cost by replacing manual trial-by-trial labeling with vision-language-model-based success assessment.
Robustness under distribution shift measures whether performance remains stable outside the training distribution. 
REALM~\cite{sedlacek2025realm} evaluates this under visual, semantic, and physical perturbations, reporting both the OOD success-rate gap, $\Delta SR_{\text{OOD}} = SR_{\text{in-dist}} - SR_{\text{out-dist}}$, and Root Mean Square Deviation to characterize degradation under shift.
Proxy validity concerns whether scalable evaluation environments preserve the same conclusions as real-world execution. SIMPLER~\cite{li2025simpler} measures this through Mismatched Ranking Violations,
\begin{equation}
\mathrm{MMRV} = \frac{1}{\binom{N}{2}} \sum_{i<j} \mathbb{1}\!\left[\mathrm{rank}_{\mathrm{sim}}(i,j) \neq \mathrm{rank}_{\mathrm{real}}(i,j)\right]
\end{equation}
which quantifies how often policy rankings disagree between simulation and physical deployment. 
WorldArena~\cite{shang2026worldarena} extends this idea to learned world models by asking whether they can serve as reliable evaluators in addition to simulators. 
Together, these methods define a hierarchy of increasingly scalable but increasingly approximate proxies, from the real world to simulators to learned world models.

\subsection{Deployment Efficiency Readiness Evaluation}\label{sec:eval_energy}

Deployment efficiency readiness concerns whether the embodied capability produced by \(B\) and \(P\) from \(\mathcal{K}\) can be executed efficiently on physical embodiments under real deployment constraints. Relevant costs include latency, compute, memory, hardware load, and energy consumption. Existing evaluation efforts in this direction can be grouped into three categories: energy decomposition, normalized efficiency comparison, and multi-objective trade-off analysis.
Existing energy-oriented evaluations usually begin with simplified power models or their proxies before moving to more structured decompositions.
Another structured view is provided by ECDP~\cite{heredia2023ecdp}, which decomposes total power into electronics, friction, copper loss, and useful mechanical work:
\begin{equation}
P_{\text{total}}(t) = P_{\text{elec}} + P_{\text{friction}}(t) + P_{\text{copper}}(t) + P_{\text{mech}}(t)
\end{equation}
This decomposition is useful because it attributes where energy is spent rather than reporting only a single aggregate number. It also shows that control electronics can dominate total consumption, which implies that trajectory optimization alone may have limited impact on deployment efficiency.
For comparison across robots, recent work introduces normalized efficiency metrics~\cite{vidussi2021energy,heredia2025evaluating} and measures such as Cost of Transport, while multi-objective analyses make explicit the trade-offs among energy, execution time, and motion quality~\cite{rezali2025lstm}. Even so, deployment cost remains only partially quantified in current embodied manipulation benchmarks, since latency, runtime reliability, compute footprint, and hardware load are still rarely reported in comparable ways.

Overall, existing evaluation efforts do not suffice to close the validation loop across the overall pipeline.
A comprehensive characterization of how $\mathcal{D}$, $\mathcal{K}$, embodiment, and the organization of \(B\) and \(P\) determine behavior under real deployment conditions is lacking.

\section{Open Challenges and Future Directions}\label{sec:challenges}

Building on the foregoing organizational framework, we examine the open challenges of embodied manipulation through the constraint chain developed above.
We show how limitations originating in embodied foundations accumulate in data support, intensify in architectural design, and ultimately make it difficult for evaluation to provide stable feedback to upstream stages. 
On this basis, we then examine safety and deployment of deployed embodied models, where additional problems emerge under coupled constraints beyond those discussed above.
Finally, we conclude the section with a brief discussion of several emerging directions.

\subsection{Embodiment Constraints}\label{sec:challenge_embodiment}

Current embodied systems can only observe and act within a narrow, hardware-specific space. 
As a result, \(\mathcal{D}\) captures only a partial view of the world and its interaction structure, rather than the full range of physically realizable perception-action couplings required for manipulation. 
This embodiment constraint defines the scope within which current robotic manipulation is formulated, collected, and benchmarked. 
Consequently, most existing work remains largely confined to visual observation modalities, sometimes augmented with depth, with only limited support for richer embodied modalities.
Moreover, because mechanical parameters, design standards, control interfaces, and even data formats often differ across platforms, the spaces they induce are not directly equivalent.
Current methods therefore rely on compensatory structural biases to turn partial, embodiment-specific evidence into usable capabilities.
These limitations can often be alleviated by strengthening data support or by adopting stronger learning paradigms, although neither fully eliminates the underlying transfer costs, and both are costly in practice.

\subsection{Data Support}\label{sec:challenge_data}

A more complete \(\mathcal{K}\) requires broader coverage in \(\mathcal{D}\), so that motion sequences, state transitions, sensory observations, and contact events are all captured more fully, stably, and reproducibly.
Direct real-world data collection can provide enough data coverage, but it remains slow, expensive, and strongly restrained to embodiment constraints. 
More scalable forms of autonomous data collection can alleviate this bottleneck, but they are reliable only for interactions that can be repeated, reset, and executed safely at scale.
Therefore, autonomous collection datasets are unable to provide sufficient coverage for state transitions and contact events in \(\mathcal{D}\).
Simulation and synthetic datasets are often easier and cheaper to generate, but \(\mathcal{K}\) they induce is often misaligned with real-world manipulation. 
Physics engines still face substantial difficulty in accurately modeling contact, friction, compliance, and deformable dynamics.
These limitations are especially evident in important real-world settings such as failures, recoveries, contact-rich transitions, and cross-embodiment adaptation.
As a result, whether simulated data can provide reliable support for downstream manipulation learning remains unresolved.

\subsection{Architectural Design}\label{sec:challenge_learning}

Current learning paradigms for robotic manipulation are built under the structural biases imposed by embodied foundations, while their execution capabilities are derived from incomplete and often unreliable \(\mathcal{K}\).
Structural biases induced by embodied foundations prevent the field from converging on a single dominant architecture, so that architectural design tends to become more complex, more heterogeneous, and more tightly coupled to embodied constraints.
Incompleteness \(\mathcal{K}\) makes learned capability difficult to organize without significant trade-offs, so that no single paradigm can optimize abstraction, executability, efficiency, and transfer at once.
In practice, different kinds of architectural designs including B,P integration models are suffering substantial trade-offs.
Even within a single architectural design, trade-offs are inevitable. 
Broad and increasingly blurred categories such as VA, VLA, WM, VAM, and WAM are proliferating across the field, 
and their definitions are becoming increasingly unstable and variably specified across contexts.
Moreover, the field is increasingly organizing heterogeneous architectural choices through localized single methods such as UWM, LAM, LAPA, ViLLA and so on.
These pressures make learning design increasingly heterogeneous, complex, and difficult to organize under stable criteria. 
As a result, the field increasingly exhibits the characteristics of system engineering.
Even after model construction, making a system workable in the real world requires substantial further adaptation, and these adaptations are themselves complex, interdependent, and often mutually incompatible.

\subsection{Evaluation}\label{sec:challenge_evaluation}

Existing metrics have become more fine-grained for execution quality, data efficiency, and transfer validity, but they are still concentrated around a relatively narrow set of task formats and embodiments. 
Execution metrics are still largely tailored to single-arm tabletop manipulation, sample-efficiency metrics often emphasize learning outcome over robustness, and sim-to-real proxies remain weakly calibrated under embodiment shift, contact-rich dynamics, and long-horizon execution.
As a result, existing evaluation provides only a partial view of the capabilities that matter for real-world deployment.
Existing benchmark environments remain largely task-specific and are often built around preferred task formats, observation settings, and evaluation criteria. 
As a result, they tend to privilege capabilities that are easy to standardize and compare, while offering only limited visibility into the robustness, transferability, and long-horizon interaction skills required in real deployment. 
Once a small set of such benchmark suites becomes dominant, these built-in preferences begin to shape the evaluation ecosystem more broadly. 
Research often adapts to their task distributions, observation formats, and success criteria, which can in turn steer model development toward benchmark compatibility over the broader capabilities required for embodied manipulation.
Real-world evaluation poses a separate reproducibility challenge. 
Results collected in different laboratories are rarely directly comparable, even when task descriptions appear similar, because embodiment, calibration, sensor placement, scene layout, intervention policy, and environmental conditions often differ substantially. 
Without stronger shared physical protocols and standardized real-world test procedures, validation on physical robots is still fragmented and often difficult to reproduce across settings.
Recent efforts such as ManipArena~\cite{sun2026maniparena} make the problem explicit: existing evaluation remains split between simulator-centric benchmarks that are controllable but deployment-incomplete, and real-world testing that is more realistic but still fragmented and weakly reproducible across platforms.

\subsection{Trustworthiness Under Coupled Constraints}\label{sec:challenge_safety}

Even when the tight coupling of embodiment, data, and learning is realized in a deployed embodied system, trustworthiness remains a distinct and unresolved challenge.
The tight coupling of embodiment, data, and learning introduces distinctive trustworthiness challenges in learned embodied manipulation systems for robotic arms.
BadVLA indicates that apparent task competence is insufficient to guarantee trustworthy deployment. 
VLA-style embodied systems can maintain strong clean-task performance while exhibiting backdoor-triggered control deviations~\cite{zhou2025badvla}. 
More broadly, this implies that trustworthiness in embodied systems must be assessed not only in terms of task completion, but also in terms of whether semantically hidden failures can propagate into untrusty physical execution.
Policy learning paradigms expose limited visibility into how perception is translated into action. 
When failures arise, it is often unclear whether the problem lies in observation encoding, reasoning, action representation, or interface mismatch with downstream controllers~\cite{liu2024runtime}. 
Hierarchical and chain-of-thought-style paradigms provide more visible intermediates, but these intermediates are not guaranteed to faithfully reflect the true basis of the final decision. 
A further challenge concerns how embodied paradigms interact with humans in shared environments. 
Trustworthiness depends not only on task success, but also on whether people can anticipate system behavior, interpret its intentions, and intervene safely when needed. 
Current paradigms still offer limited support for such calibrated collaboration, raising questions about responsibility, liability, and acceptable deployment boundaries.

\subsection{Emerging Directions}\label{sec:future_directions}

Recent work suggests that the field is beginning to move beyond isolated module-level improvements toward broader cross-stage directions. 
On the embodiment and platform side, platforms such as UniDex~\cite{zhang2026unidex} highlight a shift toward embodiment-aware frameworks that unify data generation, cross-embodiment policy learning, and evaluation for dexterous manipulation.
IAIL~\cite{chen2026mint}, by contrast, aligns behavior at the level of intention, allowing robots with different morphologies to learn from one another without requiring trajectory-level correspondence.
At the same time, data pipelines are becoming more reusable. 
Always-on egocentric capture~\cite{yang2026aoe} explores how everyday human activity can be turned into a scalable data stream without dedicated recording sessions, 
while correspondence-driven trajectory warping~\cite{liang2026tether} shows how single demonstrations can be adapted across novel scenes without relying on a physics engine. 
Together, these developments point toward embodied systems that depend less on narrowly collected, platform-specific experience and more on representations and data pipelines that transfer across hardware, environments, and tasks.
The relationship between learning and deployment is also changing. 
Embodied-R1~\cite{liu2025embodiedr1} shows that progress in reasoning and action modeling can matter as much as scale, 
while VLANeXt~\cite{chen2026vlanext} shows that principled architectural design within a unified embodied architecture can also lead to substantial performance gains.
PF-DAG~\cite{lei2026pfdag} shows that hybrid policy design is a promising direction, decoupling coarse mode selection from fine-grained continuous action generation.
WoVR~\cite{huang2026wovr} and ReinFlow~\cite{yuan2025reinflow} move beyond static pretraining through world-model-based post-training and online reinforcement learning. 
These studies suggest that embodied agents can continue to adapt after deployment.
As embodied systems move closer to real-world use, evaluation rigor and deployment trustworthiness are increasingly important.
RoboChallenge~\cite{wang2025robochallenge} advances standardized large-scale real-robot evaluation, while SafeVLA~\cite{zhang2025safevla} and recent work on mechanistic interpretability~\cite{haon2025mechinterp} emphasize reliability, robustness, and transparency under deployment conditions. 

\section{Conclusion}

This survey organizes learned manipulation intelligence for robotic arms into four interdependent stages: hardware embodiment, data ecosystem, learning paradigm, and evaluation. 
These stages respectively define the physical basis of manipulation, the data conditions for acquiring intelligence, the learning mechanisms that coordinate perception, planning, and control, and the criteria by which progress is measured. 
Together, they show that embodied manipulation cannot be understood through model categories alone. We hope this taxonomy helps clarify these dependencies and provide a clearer basis for future work in embodied manipulation.
